title: top: do not depend on ps(1) in container
body:
This ended up more complicated then expected. Lets start first with the
problem to show why I am doing this:

Currently we simply execute ps(1) in the container. This has some
drawbacks. First, obviously you need to have ps(1) in the container
image. That is no always the case especially in small images. Second,
even if you do it will often be only busybox's ps which supports far
less options.

Now we also have psgo which is used by default but that only supports a
small subset of ps(1) options. Implementing all options there is way to
much work.

Docker on the other hand executes ps(1) directly on the host and tries
to filter pids with `-q` an option which is not supported by busybox's
ps and conflicts with other ps(1) arguments. That means they fall back
to full ps(1) on the host and then filter based on the pid in the
output. This is kinda ugly and fails short because users can modify the
ps output and it may not even include the pid in the output which causes
an error.

So every solution has a different drawback, but what if we can combine
them somehow?! This commit tries exactly that.

We use ps(1) from the host and execute that in the container's pid
namespace.
There are some security concerns that must be addressed:
- mount the executable paths for ps and podman itself readonly to
  prevent the container from overwriting it via /proc/self/exe.
- set NO_NEW_PRIVS, SET_DUMPABLE and PDEATHSIG
- close all non std fds to prevent leaking files in that the caller had
  open
- unset all environment variables to not leak any into the contianer

Technically this could be a breaking change if somebody does not
have ps on the host and only in the container but I find that very
unlikely so I have removed the in container fallback.

This updates the docs accordingly, note that podman pod top never falls
back to executing ps in the container as this makes no sense with
multiple containers so I fixed the docs there as well.

Fixes https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/19001
Fixes https://bugzilla.redhat.com/show_bug.cgi?id=2215572
#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
podman top no longer depends on ps(1) being present in the container image and now uses the one from the host.
```

comments.body: @vrothberg @giuseppe PTAL
It's on my review list for Monday :+1:

@Luap99 Excellent work.
LGTM

@giuseppe PTAL, this is a bit different than you diff as I didn't liked the linker hack.
However it should still have all the security options correctly configured.

[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *giuseppe*, *Luap99* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [Luap99,giuseppe] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

/hold
There is a bug which causes a go panic because I close the fds to early and might close the ones from the goruntime.
Do you think we should not allow it with SYS_TRACE?  I am having second thoughts that it could still be abused in some cases

> Do you think we should not allow it with SYS_TRACE? I am having second thoughts that it could still be abused in some cases

But how could we enforce that? We can check for the container config but there can always be exec session created while we start executing ps. So this is a classic TOCTOU race for exec sessions, unless of course we keep the container locked while we run ps.

Only for the container configuration, if an exec session adds that capability, it is a recipe for security issues. The problem is with the container being able to look into the proc/$PID of the new process an be able to escape to the host, even in readonly mode

Ok, so only check for SYS_TRACE? Or is there some other capability as well?
And then as fallback in that case just keep using the old podman exec way?
That is the only one I am aware of, not sure without checking in the kernel if there are other setups that could allow accessing /proc/top-pid/root files from the other processes in the container
PTAL again
@giuseppe PTAL
LGTM, @Luap99 could you please just confirm on RHEL8 the main process in the container cannot read files under /proc/TOP-PID/root if you add all caps except SYS_TRACE?
You need to confirm it on the process after the execve, not while it is in the C trampoline code you've added

I tried your new code with a fedora container running sleep trying to check sched, and I see

```
 ./bin/podman top -l sched
Error: ps(1) failed with exit code 1: error: unsupported option (BSD syntax)

Usage:
 ps [options]

 Try 'ps --help <simple|list|output|threads|misc|all>'
  or 'ps --help <s|l|o|t|m|a>'
 for additional help text.

For more details see ps(1).
```

> LGTM, @Luap99 could you please just confirm on RHEL8 the main process in the container cannot read files under /proc/TOP-PID/root if you add all caps except SYS_TRACE?

I did test that by replacing ps with sleep, I cannot access that.



> I tried your new code with a fedora container running sleep trying to check sched, and I see
> 
> ```
>  ./bin/podman top -l sched
> Error: ps(1) failed with exit code 1: error: unsupported option (BSD syntax)
> 
> Usage:
>  ps [options]
> 
>  Try 'ps --help <simple|list|output|threads|misc|all>'
>   or 'ps --help <s|l|o|t|m|a>'
>  for additional help text.
> 
> For more details see ps(1).
> ```

`sched` is not a valid ps argument as it tries to parse them as BSD style args, are you trying to use `ps -o sched`?

Yes, we got a question from a customer, how to check if a container is running with the correct scheduler if running under the real-time kernel.  So I was trying out the podman top command and saw the failure.

Anyways not something to hold up this PR.

/lgtm
/hold cancel

$ man ps | grep sched
       -c     Show different scheduler information for the -l option.
       y     priority     kernel scheduling priority
       class       CLS       scheduling class of the process.  (alias policy, cls).  Field's possible values
       cls         CLS       scheduling class of the process.  (alias policy, cls).  Field's possible values
       policy      POL       scheduling class of the process.  (alias class, cls).  Possible values are:
       sched       SCH       scheduling policy of the process.  The policies SCHED_OTHER (SCHED_NORMAL),
Because the command is `ps -o sched` not `ps sched`, so use `podman top <name> -o sched`
